turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Was that a violation of the securities laws?

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: At that particular point there is a suggestion that it would be, Mr. Chief Justice. However, it was confirmed by what happened in or about april of 1980 when they contacted Mr. Neadeau directly. Mr. Neadeau was alleged to be the president of TONM, and they asked Mr. Neadeau whether this information that they had learned from the broker, Mr. Lazzaro, was true, and they were told by Mr. Neadeau as follows: first, that he could not confirm or deny that information; second, he told them why, because it was not yet public. Then he told them when it would be public. He said you will have to wait until you see the annual report. And then to make sure that there was no misunderstanding, that the information might be was a very trustworthy and a good man.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And you suggest that was a signal

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: Exactly. I suggest that the following is true, that under the analysis of this Court in Dirks, that that conduct, their subsequent trading with that information, that inside information, was in fact a violation of Section 10 of the Act, for the responsibility. As President of that company he had a fiduciary responsibility to his stockholders not to selectively tell a broker and that broker's customers so that that broker's customers could trade, that broker could earn commissions, that broker's own stock could be benefitted -- and these parties were told about the stock of Mr. Lazzaro and his family.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, Mr. Warren, is the tippee liable derivatively through Lazzaro or directly, in your

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: The tippee here, Justice through the contact with Mr. Neadeau, and derivatively by virtue of being, if you will, a subtippee from Mr. Lazzaro, knowing of Mr. Neadeau's involvement or of some counterpart of Mr. Neadeau. the face of the complaint, do we not?

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: Exactly we do.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And the complaint does not say, of course, how Mr. Lazzaro got his information or what it was that Mr. Neadeau told Mr. Lazzaro, if anything.

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: That is correct. All we know is —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: it could be a total fabrication by Mr. Lazzaro that he was told anything by Mr. Neadeau.

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: It could have been. However, what we know is that the belief --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, just looking at the face of the complaint, that could be true, could.it not?

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: That could be true. It —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And therefore, no derivative liability of the tippee through Mr. Lazzaro from the face of the complaint alone.

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: Not on the basis of what the facts are as the Plaintiffs assumed them to be. And that's what we must look at in deciding whether they either committed or attempted to commit a violation of

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And whet is it that Mr. Neadeau said directly to the tippee that you say imposes this liability on the part of the tippee?

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: First, the Plaintiffs allege, and they allege this as a charging allegation, so it is assumed that this was an important statement to them and one that they deemed important in determining to go upon this acquisition program of stock -- they said they told confirm was the gold strike in Surinam and the prospectige joint venture with St. Joe's.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I thought the complaint discloses that Mr. Neadeau refused to confirm or deny that.

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: The complaint alleges a statement by Mr. Neadeau that the Plaintiffs allege misled them into believing that they had inside information and a jump on every other TONM shareholder in that he made a it must be to make it a charging allegation -- was a clever confirmation while nonconfirming. They said that realities were that when he said the reason he couldn't public, they would have to wait for that annual report to see that, but he did want to tell them that Mr. There is only one inference from that, and if that is not -- if that is not an improper transmission by Mr. Neadeau, then in the brokerage industry I believe they are going to talk about somewbody being Neadeaulized because that is nothing more than a ploy, at least as they allege it, that's their charge, a ploy to indicate that they had inside information, an unfair jump on the TONM shareholders. And they say that they acted on it. They say in reliance on it, on the premise that they had inside information, they bought from other shareholders, and they bought at two, they bought with an advance knowledge of market conditions, and they did in fact watch the stock over a period of time from November of 1979 up to the first quarter of 1981, and indeed, in the last quarter of 1980, hit a high water mark of seven. So based upon the information, the jumps that they thought they had, they did indeed have a jump. They rode that stock from approximately two up to seven, and had they sold at that point, they would have actually achieved the results of their contemplated between these -- the tipper, the tipster and the tippee, our view, because they each intended according to the allegations of the complaint as we read them to commit a fraud on stockholders.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, they also -- they also intended to use inside information.

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: The tippees?

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: Oh, the tipster to use in side information is not true, that the tipster was passing a wrong that information.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, then, the tipster not only was purporting to use inside information, but he was also lying.

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: And says, I believe, the Dirks --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And the tippee, the tippee didn't lie to anybody, he just didn't tell them what the facts were .

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: Well, I don't -- I suggest that the difference between an actionable misrepresentation under 10(b) (5) and an actionable nondisclosure such as an affiliated ute under 10(b) (5) are indistinguishable. Obviously —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, but the tipster might be stuck under state law for defrauding somebody.

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: The tipster might be stuck under state law for defrauding somebody, of course.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He wouldn't be — but the tippee wouldn't be committing a state law offense by just not -- just by saying caveat emptor.

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: Oh, I -- perhaps I am unduly influence by the fact that I am from the State of California, but I don't think that could be --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You have statutory.

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: I don't think that could be said in the State of California.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You have statutory remedies.

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: Well, we have, yes, we have a -- yes, we are a code state. However, we have a well developed case law in California, and we have duties of when it is necessary to disclose in order not to commit fraud, and I believe that this Court has drawn upon common law concepts as to those duties to disclose. In Dirks, when we talk about there being fiduciary

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So the tippee Wants to buy some stock from some stockholder because he thinks the stock stock is going up, that would be his -- would give the

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: No * but if he, if he had gone to a corporate insider with a fiduciary duty to that shareholder that he is going to go buy it from --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I understand that. a breach of duty to do so, and he then goes and buys that stock and he doesn't disclose, yes, I believe indeed he is guilty of actual fraud. I believe he is guilty of actual fraud under common law, under state law, and under the federal securities laws.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If this stock had gone to 28 and then this fellow had sold it and made a very large profit, would it be subject to some vulnerability on the

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: Would the plaintiffs here be subject to vulnerability to a suit by others? Yes, indeed, I believe so.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Now, as I read the District judge, be set by the court In Briscoe v. LaHue, however it is correctly important purposes such as the civil rights statutes, it is presumed to be aware of the common law land the that tort structure. to Section 10 because of the savings clauses that are which accept common law remedies and deem them to continue in effect. The federal courts uniformly, in applying Section 10, have adopted common law defenses based upon the conduct of plaintiffs, for example, laches, waiver, those based upon conduct by the plaintiffs have been accepted. One of them has been in pari delicto. The federal courts have applied in pari delicto in involving the tipper/tippee type of situation. one question?

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: Yes, sir

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I guess one of the problems is what really is equal fault in cases of this kind, and if I understand your colloquy with Justice White, you say it really doesn't make any difference whether the tipster was telling the truth or not; in either event it is equally bad.

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: Yes --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is that -- in other words, supposing you had a case in which a tipster — a broker says to a customer, I just talked to the president of the company and they are about to buy a gold mine, and in one case that is just no basis in fact for it; in the other case it's totally true. Would you -- and the customer recover?

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: There's that possibility. not a false way. You say there is no difference between the ase where he tells the truth and the one where the broker lies. because there is no cause of action. is faultb on each side.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, there is intent in both cass

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And it seems to me that one could was true but they just thought they would make a little

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: I understand, Justice Stevens, but I don't think, still, in your first postulate, there parties may have engaged in inside trading, but there would be no relationship between the inside trading and the loss. In other words, the party who received the tip would not have a cause of action against the party who gave the tip because of the concept of inside trading, there would be no deception.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But there would be -- isn't it a violation of the securities law to give inside information in that situation?

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: Perhaps insofar as the SEC is concerned, but not insofar as a private right of action is concerned.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, why not? If. he acts on illegally acquired information, the stock instead of going up goes down, why hasn't he been injured?

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: Because there's no deception, there's been no violation as to him. The violation occurs in the inside information case as to the party who doesn't know about it. It's the superior knowledge improperly obtained that makes the violation.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So you are saying that the broker information to a customer, does not violate the statute? indeed to the stockholders from whom the customer buys information to a customer and is truthful as between commits a fraud upon the third party, but the customer has no claim whatsoever.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I don't understand your as to. There is or is not a violation. If the SEC could because the broker did something bad to the customer -- and it conceivably, it could cause an injury to the person who acts on the basis of the information. are having the customer come in and sue, and it is not the customer who has been misled under those circumstances. The customer has been told the truth. And if the customer has been told the truth, the customer has no claim.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, why don't we leave out all these -- why don't you just say that the insider, the tiposter is, say, an officer of the corporation.

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And he tips a friend. We are going to buy a gold mine.

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And that friend then goes out and buys a bunch of corporate stock without revealing anything, without revealing that he is dealing in inside information.

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Those people he bought the stock from have a cause of action against him.

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: They do, yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, they do. And does the tippee have a cause of action against the tipster?

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: No. the assumption --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, but that's only on the inside information at all. He has not revealed any inside information. That is just a dream world.

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: He would but for the existence of

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I know, but --

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: That's the one hypothetical --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In this case. in this case , in this pursuing before, you are comparing dealing in ins ide

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And you are trying to enforce that also through the tippee.

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: Justice White, an insider trading case is an actual fraud. It is not different from an actual fraud, it is --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I agree, I agree.

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: -- on the shareholder that sells the stock.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: The shareholder th at sells the stock without being told the truth, that' s the fraud,

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It's a breach of fid uciary duty, Is that -- you can call it that or anythi ng .

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: Well, it is a —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's fraud, a type of fraud,

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: It is a fraudulent or deceptive practice under Section 10.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And you say that's - - if that' the -- and you think that the tipster who does that suppose he just lies, as I said. Do you think that is -- that he has committed a fraud on th e stockholders?

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: No.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If he just lies?

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: If he just lies to his customer,

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: All right, he has lied to his friend, and he has committed on fraud on the

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: That's right, he has just defrauded his friend. think, though, in that case there would be in pari he intended to commit onthe customer. He is a If there is no cause of action against the defendant in the first place, we are not talking about in pari delicto, we are talking about no claim.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Warren —

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: It's clear that the plaintiffs at.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, did Dirks look at what the tippee thought, or did Dirks also speak in terms of a requirement that the tipster be acting for personal gain?

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: In the case of in pari delicto, I think w are looking at what the tippee thought, but let's add both.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So suppose the tipster just let it slip at a golf game, no personal gain at all.

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: Now, that's correct, that's correct. However, in this case, what is clear here is that Mr. Lazzaro told them that he had in side information from that officer. They called the officer, and he told them it was nonpublc, in other words, they shouldn't have it, that nobody else would receive it until later, but that Mr. Lazzaro was a very good and trustworthy man, he knew they were dealing with Lazzaro, he knew they were going to buy the stock, he knew that would benefit Lazzaro, it would make Lazzaro look like a very smart man. to see whether there was any benefit to the insider,

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, did the complaint allege that Complaint, it looks to me like you don't have in pari

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: Justice O'Connor, we look at the facts in the complaint, and those facts, while they dont personally gain. They show that he gifted or benefitted a stock broker. He selective released information in a way that would benefit that stockbroker. It alleges So the facts alleged show that personal gain. We don't need to have the complaint say it in fact was —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Could I ask you one quick question? Do we judge this case, or does the record show, or are there findings as to whether or not the tipster here conveyed both true and false information or only true or only false?

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: All right. The fact is that the comparison of I believe it is paragraphs 16 and 33 of the complaint, 16 being the representations, and 33 being the falsity, shows that we have a mixture, in other words, that they don't contend there wasn't any gold find; they quarrel with its state of development. They don't contend that there were no joint venture negotiations they simply quarrel with the stage at which those negotiations had progressed.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So you think that the Complaint on its face alleges that part of it is false and part of it is true?

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: I think that is correct. I think, on the other hand, it wouldn't make any difference whether it was all false.

turns.name: ROBERT S. WARREN
turns.text: Because as in the case of Judge you believe is criminal activity is plenty enough for in pari delicto. It is plenty enough for punishment for crime. Your Honor, if I may, I am through.

turns.name: GEOFFREY P. KNUDSEN
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it This case is not an insider trading case. It is a case in which the complaint alleged a common, the Plaintiffs. The fraud was, as the complaint alleges, was a planned -- a plan conceived by the Defendants to manipulate the price of stock in TONM, not for the benefit of all, but for the benefit of the Defendants alone. As part of the scheme, the Defendants --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That he was -- the narrowness of, the scope of the fraud. alleged in the complaint, would have some effect in terms of interpreting whether the Plaintiffs could be considered to be of equal fault or in pari delicto, but the complaint itself is meant to alleged a -- just a fraud 10(b) (5) violation in the context of a market manipulation, and that the plaintiffs were injured by it. What makes it a little bit different is that the complaint alleges that the Plaintiffs, who ended up losig their money, were utilized as an instrumentality of the Defendants' fraud. In other words, it was the information alleged to be inside to the Plaintiffs. They initiated the contact, they did it for purposes of having the Plaintiffs trade, driving the price of the stock up. They never intended the Plaintiffs to benefit from that market manipulation, and in fact, as the Complaint alleges, when the Plaintiffs indicated some desire to sell when the stock got up at its higher levels, they continued the fraud so that they would will go higher, it will go higher. So it is not a situation where the Defendants utilize inside information. It is solely a situation where the defendants are alleged to have embarked upon a scheme to defraud the market and defraud these plaintiffs .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, would the tippee in this case the information was false, there would be no liability. no actual use of inside information?

turns.name: GEOFFREY P. KNUDSEN
turns.text: No one was injured. There's nothing -- he wouldn't have a duty to disclose false

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So you think we have to read the complaint as alleging everything was false.

turns.name: GEOFFREY P. KNUDSEN
turns.text: I think you need to read the complaint as alleging everything is false. That's what the charging allegation is -- with you, part fault, part true, and so there was some thestock on the open market didn't disclose.

turns.name: GEOFFREY P. KNUDSEN
turns.text: Well, I don't think you can look at the face of the complaint and then conclude that the allegations are part false and part true. The central allegation of the complaint is paragraph 16, that the defendants were engaged in --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I know, but let me just give you my question again.

turns.name: GEOFFREY P. KNUDSEN
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Let's assume we don't agree with you and that the complaint says part true, part false. Now what about the position of the tippee vis-a-vis the person he bought stock from without disclosing?

turns.name: GEOFFREY P. KNUDSEN
turns.text: Would he have liability?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Ye s.

turns.name: GEOFFREY P. KNUDSEN
turns.text: To the extent that there damag e such a person —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So the answer is yes.

turns.name: GEOFFREY P. KNUDSEN
turns.text: Related to the true information.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So he failed to disclose, and he could be held liable under the securities laws. MR. KNUDSEN : If he received true information

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, exactly.

turns.name: GEOFFREY P. KNUDSEN
turns.text: To the extent that he receives true information, he would be delicto, in delicto.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Exactly.

turns.name: GEOFFREY P. KNUDSEN
turns.text: That's right

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And the argument would -- then the question would come down, is it in pari delicto. the face of the complaint, assuming that you read the complaint in my opinion would be a very bad device to make that determination of in pari delicto.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, but it was made below.

turns.name: GEOFFREY P. KNUDSEN
turns.text: Well, it was made by the district -- Court, and that was my argument in the 9th Circuit, that it would be a bad device to use. But the 9th Circuit of the information false. That's actually what the 9th Circuit concluded, was that

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So as far as they are concerned, there was no use of inside information at all.

turns.name: GEOFFREY P. KNUDSEN
turns.text: I don't think that they decided the issue of whether it is inside information in the context of this --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, if it was false, it wasn't.

turns.name: GEOFFREY P. KNUDSEN
turns.text: No. I think that the 9th Circuit assumes that there is a line -- there was a line of authority that says if you have a tippee attempting to trade on inside information, that he may be guilty of a violation, but concluded in the circumstances of the allegation of the complaint where an intentional fraud being perpetrated on the Plaintiffs was alleged, that more or less as a matter of law those parties could not be in pari delicto. That is not inconsistent with the common law.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I agree with you, I agree with' you.

turns.name: GEOFFREY P. KNUDSEN
turns.text: Which at common law, if you have an actual fraud and the determination, there is a public policy issue if withholding the relief would do more to offend public morals, then in pari delicto would not apply, and that is the situation that the 9th Circuit addressed and said if there is a fraud aimed at these clients and the Plaintiffs as an instrumentality of that, and they were not part of a conspiracy to benefit money, and that is why they have come forward and asserted their claims.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I don't understand how you can s«y that in response to the Chief Justice's question. You Say if they had been smart enough to sell out before it dropped, they would be liable to other parties if part of the information was truthful. At least they thought it was truthful when they were relying on the inside information, didn't they? That's why they bought it.

turns.name: GEOFFREY P. KNUDSEN
turns.text: Yes, that's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Now, why isn't that a violation? Why wouldn't they be liable to the people to whom --

turns.name: GEOFFREY P. KNUDSEN
turns.text: I said they would if the information was true information.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What difference does it make? They thought it was true at the time they engaged in the trades, didn't they? That's the whole hypothesis of the fraud. So how can their blameworthiness depend on whether the information turns out to be true or false? I don't understand.

turns.name: GEOFFREY P. KNUDSEN
turns.text: Well, I think that the hypothetical becomes a little difficult to deal with because if it is false information, the stock doesn't go to 28. That's —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, it may have as long as people gold mine over wherever this place was --

turns.name: GEOFFREY P. KNUDSEN
turns.text: Oh, I see, and they sell out in the context of -- They just —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The market goes up on the basis of public perception, not reality.

turns.name: GEOFFREY P. KNUDSEN
turns.text: That's absolutely right, and that's what is charged by the complaint. They would have liability to the extent that they received true information, and you are asking would they also have liability to those folks that sold their ’ And I would say no because —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Isn't it almost certainly true that not going to assume the burden of proving every single in these facts, there is always a mixture of truth, even in the most gross fraud. Do you assume that you have to prove that every statement in the complaint was false?

turns.name: GEOFFREY P. KNUDSEN
turns.text: No. I assume I have to prove that there was an intentional scheme to manipulate the market utilizing my clients, providing them --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And the sheme involved a mixture of —

turns.name: GEOFFREY P. KNUDSEN
turns.text: Essentially --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- half truths and truths and falsehoods.

turns.name: GEOFFREY P. KNUDSEN
turns.text: But essentially false information.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: WE11, I just have difficulty understanding how your -- whether your client was engaged in wrongdoing turns out -- depends on whether he finds out later that the information was false or not.

turns.name: GEOFFREY P. KNUDSEN
turns.text: Well, to the extent my client is engaged in wrongdoing may not depend on that at all unless you look at it in the context of perhaps this court's Chiarella and Dirks analysis, and then you have to look at a duty to disclose, and then the question comes up how can you have a -- how could the insider is the very thing you don't want to happen, but in whether it is true or false, my clients are in delicto, as Justice White said. The question becomes if they are intentionally manipulated, are they in pari delicto. In terms of the Plaintiffs' position, their question?

turns.name: KNUOSEN
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Then I will get it all off my chest. in which a plaintiff just said he got inside information which he relied on and was true and so forth and so on, Do you think the Plaintiff has a cause of action because you don't have the degree of scienter on the Defendant that would be required by Ernst & Ernst for a 10(b) violation, I agree.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He would know he is deal -- he is passing out inside information, he knows he has a duty not to do so. The customer, the broker, knows it is inside information, knows the law says you are not supposed to do this, you say there is no scienter? That's the answer?

turns.name: GEOFFREY P. KNUDSEN
turns.text: Maybe I misunderstood your question.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Everybody is telling the truth. This is my hypothesis.

turns.name: GEOFFREY P. KNUDSEN
turns.text: Everyone is telling the truth.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The broker says they just found a gold mine, they are going to make a billion dollar deal, it is a good buy, it is inside information, don't tell anybody.

turns.name: GEOFFREY P. KNUDSEN
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You trade on it, and the stock goes up for a while, but then the deal falls through, but everybody is dealing on inside information. Does the customer of the stock broker have a cause of action against the stock broker for dealing in

turns.name: GEOFFREY P. KNUDSEN
turns.text: No, I don't believe so because of the scienter requirement for -- because it is not an insider -- he doesn't have a claim for insider trading. If he has a claim against a broker for anything, it is for fraud under 10(b) (5) , and that would require scienter, which would require some intentionally false or recklessly false information imparted to him, and your hypothetical is it is true.

turns.name: GEOFFREY P. KNUDSEN
turns.text: Pardon me?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What it boils down to is it is all right to trade on inside information as long as it is true .

turns.name: GEOFFREY P. KNUDSEN
turns.text: No, that's a different question. The Plaintiff, someone trading on true inside information is going to be liable for criminal sanction. The SEC can proceed against him, and anyone selling his stock who loses, incurs injury, Can proceed against him. But that doesn't necessarily -- that's really a separate question. That doesn't necessarily intentionally misleads him and provides him Yo u see, he's not sui ng - - in that cas e you dlti not cons idering the context of the t ippee v i s-a-vis the broker . You are looking at the tipp ee vi s-a -vis the r eg ulators o r vis-a-vis someone who lost mone y t o whom h e might hav e a duty to disclos e th e cor r ect inf ormation. In any event, given the 1 eg isl ative hi story of the insider trading sane tions a ct, I thi nk th ey place the pr imary responsibili ty with the brok er s a nd the ins iders. I think this Court's dec is ion s tha t i ndicate tha t the tippee liability is de r iva t ive indie ate that the person a t the tippee level is g oing to be » i n the common law sense, even the common law sense, of in pari delicto, not of equal fault. And the facts -- thank you.

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: Mr. Kuhlik? PRO HAC VICE, FOR SECURITIES AND EXCHANGE COMMISSION, AS AMICUS CURIAE IN SUPPORT OF RESPONDENTS

turns.name: BRUCE N. KUHLIK
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: I would like to focus on what the Commission believes is the dispositive factor in this litigation, deterring violations of the federal securities laws. We think that there can be no doubt that further the deterrence of violations of Section 10(b) and Rule 10 (b) (5) . Now, deterrence of wrongdoing is an important component of the in pari delicto defense as it has should be deterred in the first instance, and we think case where private actions such as those under Rule 10(b) , or excuse me, Rule 10(b) (5) , makes an important contribution to enforcement of the federal securities laws. We think that the Court's decision in Perma Life Mufflers which held the defense unavailable in a private insiders. I don't think there can be any doubt that allowing the lawsuit to go forward will deter brokerage frauds on their clients. That is the whole purpose of the action to be implied. And the only question then becomes whether or not there is some other overriding factor on the other side in terms of perhaps encouraging wrongdoing by persons in the position of the Plaintiffs that would, as I say, override this deterrent impact on the brokers. And we think that that overriding factor on the other side just isn't present. In the Commission's view, allowing this action to go forward will not unduly encourage illegal tippee trading. In the first place, allowing the suit to go forward will deter brokers from giving information in the first place. It will cut that inside information off at the source. It won't be there for the tippees to trade on. In the second place, investors in the position of the Plaintiffs, in the Commission's view, are often unsophisticated, certainly as compared with the brokerate professionals here, it might be believed that they were unsophisticated, unschooled in the law, and we are far more likely to respond to the deterrent impact of not allowing the defense here than ordinary investors allowing the defense. And I think that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: May I ask just one question?

turns.name: BRUCE N. KUHLIK
turns.text: Yes, Justice Stevens.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Assuming after trial there is a

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, does the Plaintiff recover

turns.name: BRUCE N. KUHLIK
turns.text: If all of the information was truthful —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It's all inside information, but it was truthful.

turns.name: BRUCE N. KUHLIK
turns.text: Then I do not believe they could recover because they would not have been deceived. will go ahead end trade on it if they believe it. 1 information believing there to be a substantial possibility that the whole information is false, simply on the chance, just the chance that they might recover later in a legal action.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So your submission is that we ought to just, in this case at least, forget the notion of equal fault?

turns.name: BRUCE N. KUHLIK
turns.text: No, Justice White, I believe --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I should say that no matter whether they are equal or not, enforcement of the securities laws is so important that the defense should not be allowed.

turns.name: BRUCE N. KUHLIK
turns.text: My position is that the enforcement of the securities laws is so important that the defense should only be allowed where it will further * the purposes of the securities laws, even if in a particular case the fault of the parties were relatively equal. But I might add that, you know, I will get to this. We don't believe that this is even a close case as to equal fault. We think it is quite clear that the position in the scheme of the capital market system and

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, the tippees, assuming there

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Under the securities law. Justice White, if a plaintiff could prove that the that that caused a trading loss to some other group of investors, then they would be liable, but — tipster did? factors. Because of their special position, but also because they are the people who instigated the fraud plaintiff investors, engaged them in a scheme, abused their position of trust to make them believe that they were trading on inside information when in fact it was the plaintiffs all along who were the intended victims, excuse me, and the actual victims of the fraud, and in this regard, I might add that the brokers and insiders such as those here, in the Commission's view, have a much greater opportunity to jeopardize the integrity of the market systems. They are continually in a position to come into inside information, to represent or to misrepresent to their clients, to their friends, to their investors that they have access to this sort of information. And the brokerage professionals such as Lazzaro here occupy a key position. They control access to these markets . If a plaintiff wants to trade, he has to do it through a broker, and it is up to the brokers to be — they are required to be conversant with the law, to be conversant with Commission rules, regulations and the statute, and it is up to them in the first instance to guide the trading of their clients into realistic way for most of these frauds to come to light is through private actions such as these, and I think there can be no doubt that the investors would have little incentive indeed to bring these sorts of actions investment, it is most, most unlikely that these sorts of frauds would be uncovered. position of the parties here, that in the Commission's view, the in pari delicto defense should be available in the investors, have instigated an illegal scheme, are truly involved in a conspiracy with a broker for their mutual benefit. But that is not what we have here. comparative fault of the parties. The Petitioner claims that under the common circumstances

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Could I ask you in this connection, please, if all the information that the broker and the insider handed out was false, all of it was false, and they knew it to be false, and they passed it out to the investor who acted on it thinking it was true inside information, he buys stock, the stock goes down, is then the investor liable to the people he bought from?

turns.name: BRUCE N. KUHLIK
turns.text: If all of the information is false, Justice White, I don't believe that the other parties to the trade would have an action.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, because there really, he wasn't using inside information.

turns.name: BRUCE N. KUHLIK
turns.text: But he -- there was nothing that he used that he should have disclosed to them that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Exactly.

turns.name: BRUCE N. KUHLIK
turns.text: -- caused them the loss in those circumstances .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes. In that event, he wouldn't be in delicto at all.

turns.name: BRUCE N. KUHLIK
turns.text: Well, he might not be, although I believe —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What would he be?

turns.name: BRUCE N. KUHLIK
turns.text: Well, some of the cases, Justice White, speak in terms of moral blame -- view is that he wouldn't be in delicto at all.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So, but he — but certainly his rights were violated at the outset.

turns.name: BRUCE N. KUHLIK
turns.text: I might have misspoken, Justice White. In our view, if all of the information were false, then another party to a trade wouldn't have had a information to be true, they belived it to be inside insider's duty, or they had notice of such a breach, the Commision most certainly could bring an action, civil or criminal action for an attempted violation of the law. So in that respect, even if all of the information is false, it may well be that the investors here have attempted to violate the law.

turns.name: BRUCE N. KUHLIK
turns.text: At this posture of the case, we don't believe the complaint conclusively shows that. simply, or it is not enough now, as the common law has fraud. A more refined balancing takes place that takes into account the deterrent purposes of the law and the actual degree of wrongdoing. And we cite a number of cases in our briefs where, for example, a plaintiff who believed he was defrauding others by engaging in a fixed foot race was allowed to recover, notwithstanding the in pari delicto doctrine, from the parties who had defrauded him, because the race in fact was fixed against him. Finally, I would note that we believe it makes little sense to adopt a rule that would distinguish solely on the basis of whether the investors had noticed that the information they were trading on was inside or indeed was information obtained in breach of an insider's duty because we wouldn't want to allow brokers to escape liability simply by couching their tips in terms of inside information. If there is nothing further --

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: Thank you, gentlemen. The case is -submitted.
